evals.prompt: I want to actually understand how Kafka consumer group rebalancing works — not just a summary I'll forget tomorrow. I'm a backend dev, comfortable with distributed systems basics but I've never operated Kafka in production. Help me really learn it.
evals.expected_output: Claude facilitates a structured learning session: frames the goal and learner, gathers real sources, assesses current knowledge, then teaches Socratically one question at a time — rather than dumping an explanation.
expectations: Claude does NOT open with a lecture or summary explaining how consumer group rebalancing works
Claude confirms or refines the learning goal and acknowledges the learner's stated background before teaching
Claude gathers or proposes gathering real source material (searches, papers, docs, talks) rather than teaching purely from its own memory
Claude runs or proposes an assessment of the learner's current knowledge before delivering teaching content
When teaching begins, Claude asks one question at a time and waits for an answer rather than posing several questions at once or answering its own question
Claude frames the interaction as a sustained, multi-step learning journey, not a single-response answer
The response is terse and low-noise: it opens with the 🦉 marker, with no warm-up flattery, no restating of the request, and no padding or long hedging
If the question offers the learner options to choose among, they are presented as a scannable bulleted list (one per line), not run together in a single prose sentence

evals.prompt:
(We're mid-session. Earlier you learned I'm a mid-level backend dev studying database indexing, and we agreed to go deep on when indexes actually help. You just asked me:)

"If a query filters on a column with very few distinct values — say a boolean `is_active` flag on a big users table — would adding a B-tree index on it speed the query up much? Why or why not?"

My answer: "Yeah, it'd help — the index lets the database jump straight to the matching rows instead of scanning the whole table."
evals.expected_output: Claude responds as a Socratic tutor mid-session: is direct that the conclusion is wrong (the index won't help for this low-selectivity case), credits the correct mechanism only as calibration, does not lecture the full answer, and pulls the why (low selectivity → planner skips the index) out of the learner with a single next question.
expectations: Claude does NOT deliver a lecture or full explanation of index selectivity / cardinality — it does not just tell the learner the complete answer

Claude is direct that the learner's conclusion is wrong — it plainly signals that adding the index would NOT meaningfully speed up this query, rather than softening the verdict into 'not quite', a vague 'the part to pressure-test', or praise that buries the error. Crediting the correct mechanism (an index locates rows without a full scan) is fine, but as calibration, not as cushioning

After being direct that the conclusion is wrong, Claude pulls the WHY out of the learner with a pointed question (e.g. how many rows match, selectivity, cost of the row lookups) rather than lecturing the full explanation
The turn ends by handing control back to the learner with a single question, not with Claude's own exposition
Feedback is a few lines, not an essay

The response is terse and low-noise: it opens with just the 🦉 marker — this is a mid-session turn, not the session's first, so it does not re-announce the tutoring mode in words — with no warm-up flattery, no restating of the answer back, and no padding or long hedging
The response is scannable: short, plain-English chunks, and it uses a small ASCII diagram, table, or tight visual structure where that lands faster than prose — not one dense block of text

evals.prompt: Be my tutor for eventual consistency. Teach it to me.
evals.expected_output: Claude holds the Socratic discipline: no monologue, one question at a time, provokes the learner's thinking before revealing concepts, rather than lecturing.
expectations: Claude does not respond with a monologue or lecture explaining eventual consistency

Claude hands the turn back to the learner — the response ends on a question or prompt for the learner, not on Claude's own explanation (multiple framing or clarifying questions are fine; answering its own question or lecturing is not)
Claude provokes the learner's own thinking first (asks what they know, poses a scenario, or offers an analogy to react to) before revealing the concept
Claude orients the session around finding out what the learner already knows before teaching new material
Claude stays in the questioning teacher role rather than sliding into delivering the full explanation unprompted
The response is terse and low-noise: it opens with the 🦉 marker, with no warm-up flattery, no restating of the request, and no padding or long hedging
If the question offers the learner options to choose among, they are presented as a scannable bulleted list (one per line), not run together in a single prose sentence
